text.content:
When the whole party works together to overcome a big challenge — crossing a vast desert, rallying a village for war, performing a complex ritual — the Director may call for a montage test. Think of it as a cinematic montage where each hero gets a moment in the spotlight.

How It Works

A montage test is a team challenge that plays out over two rounds. Every hero takes a turn each round, and the group tracks total successes and failures toward a shared goal. If you hit the success limit before the failure limit (or before time runs out), you win.

The Director sets the scene and describes the obstacles you face — treacherous terrain, hostile weather, suspicious crowds, ticking clocks. These descriptions are your cue for what kinds of tests you might attempt.

Your Options Each Turn

On your turn in a montage test round, you choose one of the following:

Option What You Do
Make a Test Describe how you tackle one of the obstacles. The Director assigns a characteristic and difficulty. A success adds to the group total; a failure adds to the failure total.
Assist Another Hero Help a teammate with their test using the standard assist rules. Your turn is spent on the assist rather than your own test.
Use an Ability or Item If you have a clever use for an ability, spell, or item, the Director may grant automatic successes with no roll required.
Do Nothing Pass your turn. Sometimes the wisest choice if you lack a relevant skill and fear adding a failure to the tally.

Key Rules to Remember

  • No repeat skills. You cannot use the same skill twice in a montage test. If you used Navigate in round 1, pick something different in round 2. Other heroes can still use Navigate — the restriction is per character.
  • Tackle new obstacles. Each test should address a challenge that has not already been resolved, unless the Director says a hazard is recurring (ongoing fire, repeated terrain, etc.).
  • Be creative. Any characteristic and skill combination can work if the Director agrees it makes sense. A Might test using Lift to haul gear over dunes, an Intuition test using Nature to predict sandstorms, a Presence test using Music to inspire the group — all valid approaches.
  • Edges and banes apply normally. Particularly clever or well-described ideas may even auto-succeed with no roll.

Outcomes

The montage test ends when the success limit or failure limit is reached, or after two rounds — whichever comes first.

Outcome What Happens
Total Success You achieve your goal without complications. You arrive ahead of the enemy, the ritual completes perfectly, the village is fully prepared.
Partial Success You succeed, but with a cost or complication. You arrive just as the enemy does, the ritual works but draws unwanted attention, the village is ready but morale is shaky.
Total Failure You do not achieve your goal. The situation becomes harder and more interesting — but the story always continues.

Victories: A total success earns the party 1 Victory (easy or moderate test) or 2 Victories (hard test). A partial success on a moderate or hard test earns 1 Victory.

Tips for Players

  • Play to your strengths. Use skills and characteristics where you have the best bonuses. A fury hauling gear, a shadow scouting ahead, a conduit navigating by instinct — everyone has something to contribute.
  • Coordinate. Talk with your party about who is tackling which obstacle. Assists are valuable if one hero has a much better chance of success.
  • Think beyond combat skills. Montage tests reward the full breadth of your character. Crafting, lore, interpersonal, and exploration skills all shine here.
  • Passing is OK. If you genuinely cannot contribute without risking a failure, doing nothing is a legitimate choice — especially late in a tight montage.
